title: IPv6 relay via SOCKS5 sends malformed domain ATYP
description:
When the relay URL contains an IPv6 address (e.g. wss://[::1]:8443/ws), parse_relay_addr returns the host string as '[::1]' — with the RFC 3986 brackets still included.

This host string is then passed to Socks5Stream::connect as the second argument's &str. tokio-socks IntoTargetAddr for (&str, u16) first tries '[::1]'.parse::<IpAddr>(), which FAILS because Rust's IpAddr parser does not accept bracket notation. It falls through to TargetAddr::Domain('[::1]', port), and the SOCKS5 CONNECT request is sent with ATYP=0x03 (domain name) containing the literal string '[::1]'.

This is malformed per RFC 1928: domain name ATYP must contain a valid hostname, not a bracketed IPv6 literal. Tor and most SOCKS5 proxies will reject the connection with a general failure code. The caller receives an opaque socks error rather than a clear 'IPv6 not supported in this form' message.

Reproduction: call open_authenticated_ws with relay URL 'wss://[::1]:8443/ws' and any socks5h:// proxy.

Fix: in parse_relay_addr (and symmetrically in parse_proxy_addr), after extracting the host string, strip leading '[' and trailing ']' if both are present, yielding a bare IPv6 address that IpAddr::parse and tokio-socks IntoTargetAddr will correctly handle as TargetAddr::Ip.
